steps.title: Find the key signal
steps.content:
The researchers concluded that REM sleep is not homeostatically regulated in fur seals.

If homeostatic regulation existed, all seals deprived of REM in water should show a consistent, significant increase in REM sleep after returning to land—to compensate for the deprivation.

The evidence must show they failed to demonstrate this predicted pattern.

steps.title: Match the evidence to the conclusion
steps.content:
Choice C states the seals “didn't consistently demonstrate a significant increase in REM sleep after their period of deprivation.” Looking at the graph, the three seals show highly variable patterns: Seal A remains elevated but Seal B drops from ~155% to ~90%, and Seal C stays near baseline.

This inconsistency—some seals increasing, others decreasing or stable—is the opposite of what homeostatic regulation predicts.

  • A discusses day-to-day consistency within each seal
  • B addresses baseline differences unrelated to the deprivation effect
  • D claims no difference at all between water and land (contradicted by the elevation visible in the graph)
.

C directly explains why the seals' response contradicts homeostatic regulation: no consistent significant increase across the group. That matches choice C — C is correct.
